SUMMARY:
A systematic review and meta-analysis of early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in low-and-middle-income countries - ScienceDirect The aim of this study was to examine the evidence for early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in children below five years in LAMI countries through a systematic review and meta-analysis. A review indicated that 81 countries implement national early childhood intervention for children with developmental delays, disabilities, and disorders; and a majority (n=47; 58%) of these programs are in LAMI countries that underscores the importance of early childhood intervention (Black et al., 2017). In this backdrop, this study primarily aimed to examine the evidence for early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in children aged 0–5 years in LAMI countries.

A systematic review and meta-analysis of early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in low-and-middle-income countries
================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Sapna V.Kumar a, Shweta Narayan a, Palash Kumar Malo b, Binukumar Bhaskarapillai b, Harish Thippeswamy c, Geetha Desai c, M. Thomas Kishore d

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajp.2022.103026](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajp.2022.103026 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1876201822000247&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •This [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with meta-analysis covered studies published during 2010-19 on early developmental intervention. 
*   •Despite methodological differences, there is evidence for positive child outcomes with early intervention programs. 
*   •Early intervention improved the home environment and responsive care for the children. 
*   •Early intervention programmes improved language, cognitive and motor abilities as noted on Bayley scales. 

Abstract
--------

Early acknowledgment of developmental difficulties in [young children](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/toddlers "Learn more about young children from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strengthens both preventive and therapeutic approaches. Despite their feasibility, early intervention services are limited in Low-and-Middle-Income (LAMI) countries compared to high income countries. The aim of this study was to examine the evidence for early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in children below five years in LAMI countries through a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and meta-analysis. In this background, original studies published in peer-reviewed journals from 2010 to 2019 with a focus on developmental difficulties including delays and deviances; randomized research design with a clear description of the intervention and measurable outcomes, and conducted in LAMI countries were considered for the [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Electronic databases, including ProQuest, [PubMed](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/medline "Learn more about PubMed from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Ovid, EBSCOhost, and Google Scholar, were searched using a combination of specific keywords. PRISMA guidelines were followed to include the studies. Each of the selected study was assessed for quality before applying appropriate statistics to synthesize the data. Fourteen publications were identified from 2697 publications for the [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), out of which four were found compatible for a meta-analysis. The studies reviewed were conducted in Bangladesh, [India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/india "Learn more about India from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Pakistan, [Uganda](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/uganda "Learn more about Uganda from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Vietnam, and Zambia. A meta-analysis of four studies provided considerable evidence for the effects of early developmental intervention. This [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") provides an evidence for early childhood intervention programs in LAMI countries. Implications of these findings for the early childhood programs are discussed in this article.

Introduction
------------

Nearly one-third of the world population of children live in low-and-middle-income (LAMI) countries. Many children do not develop their full potential due to poverty, low income, poor nutrition, inadequate psychosocial stimulation, and low awareness of developmental deviances or early intervention at the community level (Black et al., 2017). Families of children with developmental difficulties including delays and disabilities depend mostly on the mainstream health care systems for appropriate interventions. However, in most LAMI countries, the health care systems are already strained due to a huge gap between demand and supply; and there is a need for sustainable models to promote early child development and prevent developmental difficulties (Ertem and World Health Organization, 2012).

Prevalence estimates of children with developmental difficulties in LAMI countries vary substantially depending on the definition and measures used to assess the nature of delay and disability (Global Research on Developmental Disabilities Collaborators, 2018). A survey indicates that the total number of children with developmental disabilities under age 5 years was 52·9 million (i.e. 8·4% of the population) out of which 95% lived in LAMI (Global Research on Developmental Disabilities Collaborators, 2018). The Global Burden of Disease estimated the number of children aged 0–14 years experiencing ‘moderate or severe disability’ at 93 million (5.1%), with 13 million (0.7%) children experiencing severe difficulties, and a majority of them living in LAMI countries (World Health Organization, 2008). A community-based, multi-stage study from India observed a prevalence rate of 2.5% for developmental delay, deviation, deformity, or disability among children under five years of age (Nair et al., 2009). An analysis using UNICEF's caregiver-reported Early Childhood Development Index found that 36.8% of 3–4 year old children in 35 LAMI countries did not attain optimum cognitive and socio-emotional skills. The problems were partly attributed to the quality of care within the child’s home environment, community and early childhood education (McCoy et al., 2016); and poverty and nutrition deficiencies (Black et al., 2017).

The first five years of age is a critical developmental period for children, during which time they develop the dynamic capacity to benefit from new learning experiences (Boivin et al., 2013). Early recognition of developmental difficulties in young children and modifying the preventable causes is still a challenge in many LAMI countries (Nelson 3rd et al., 2019; Persha et al., 2007; Rout et al., 2008; Black et al., 2017). Though the developmental problems may have a biological basis, psychosocial approaches are relevant because they encompass many modifiable factors such as quality of child care, home environment, alleviation of poverty, and learning opportunities (Persha et al., 2007, Levy, 2018). A review indicated that 81 countries implement national early childhood intervention for children with developmental delays, disabilities, and disorders; and a majority (n=47; 58%) of these programs are in LAMI countries that underscores the importance of early childhood intervention (Black et al., 2017). Despite theoretical basis, evidence for early intervention programs is not available in most LAMI countries.

Given that there is ample scope for scaling up effective interventions targeting the most vulnerable children (Lu et al., 2016), there is a need to understand what effective programs are available in LAMI countries. In this backdrop, this study primarily aimed to examine the evidence for early childhood intervention programs for developmental difficulties in children aged 0–5 years in LAMI countries. The objectives were to describe the nature of interventions and their outcomes in the target groups.

----------------

Data Sources
------------

We developed the search strategy through a consultative process with mental health clinicians working in early child development. Accordingly, appropriate free terms and MeSH terms were identified, condensed, and adjusted with the database search. The keywords used were: Infants, toddlers, early childhood, developmental vulnerabilities, developmental problems, developmental disabilities, developmental delays, developmental deviances, developmental difficulties, early intervention, and

Inclusion of studies
--------------------

Electronic search yielded 2697 results, with additional 33 records identified through references of the searched articles. OVID and EBSCOhost led to PubMed or ProQuest. After removing the duplicates, 65 articles were identified. When the title and abstracts were reviewed, 30 studies were found not to have met the inclusion criteria. The full-text articles of the remaining 35 were reviewed. A randomization study from India (Deepa and Sharada Devi, 2017) was excluded from the final list because

Discussion
----------

This systematic review, and meta-analysis were undertaken in the background of existing evidence, which indicates that a large proportion of children under five years in LAMI countries are at risk for not attaining their full developmental potential (Black et al., 2017, Durkin, 2019, Global Research on Developmental Disabilities Collaborators, 2018). Cited by (12)
-------------

*   ### [Predictive validity of ESSENCE Q screening tool for early detection of neurodevelopmental disorder in children](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1876201822001605)

2022, Asian Journal of Psychiatry Citation Excerpt : 
As such, this screening tool is useful in detecting young children having developmental difficulties. In addition, there is emerging evidence of the beneficial effects of early childhood intervention programs in the LAMIC for developmental difficulties (Kumar et al., 2022). For example, the authors noted that early interventions that strengthen positive parenting practices, parent-child interaction patterns, playful learning activities, and developmentally appropriate activities are beneficial.   Show abstract Neurodevelopmental disorders (NDD) are a group of conditions that typically manifest early during the child’s development with lifelong consequences. Early identification using efficient screening tools can positively modify the natural history of the disorder. ESSENCE Q is a simple questionnaire to detect ESSENCE (Early Symptomatic Syndromes Eliciting Neurodevelopmental Clinical Examinations to reflect the co-existence of NDDs in children less than five years). There are limited studies on the validity of ESSENCE Q in detecting NDDs in young children in India. We did a cross-sectional study in a tertiary care hospital to evaluate the validity of ESSENCE Q in detecting neurodevelopmental disorders. We translated the ESSENCE Q and subsequently used it to screen for NDD in 100 children aged 12–60 months. Clinical consensus diagnosis by two experienced experts was taken as the gold standard. 23% were diagnosed as having NDD as per the clinical consensus diagnosis. Around half the children (46%) were “at-risk for NDD” based on the ESSENCE Q scale. We found an optimal cut-off for ESSENCE Q of more than or equal to 4 with a sensitivity of 96%, a specificity of 82%, and a Youden index of 0.77. ESSENCE Q has good predictive validity to be used as a quick and easy screening tool to detect NDDs in young children under the age of 5 years

# Early Intervention for Children at High Risk of Developmental Disability in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Narrative Review

### Affiliations

# Early Intervention for Children at High Risk of Developmental Disability in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Narrative Review

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

In low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), while neonatal mortality has fallen, the number of children under five with developmental disability remains unchanged. The first thousand days are a critical window for brain development, when interventions are particularly effective. Early Childhood Interventions (ECI) are supported by scientific, human rights, human capital and programmatic rationales. In high-income countries, it is recommended that ECI for high-risk infants start in the neonatal period, and specialised interventions for children with developmental disabilities as early as three months of age; more data is needed on the timing of ECI in LMICs. Emerging evidence supports community-based ECI which focus on peer support, responsive caregiving and preventing secondary morbidities. A combination of individual home visits and community-based groups are likely the best strategy for the delivery of ECI, but more evidence is needed to form strong recommendations, particularly on the dosage of interventions. More data on content, impact and implementation of ECI in LMICs for high-risk infants are urgently needed. The development of ECI for high-risk groups will build on universal early child development best practice but will likely require tailoring to local contexts.

**Keywords:**
developmental disability; early childhood intervention; low- and middle-income countries; newborn.

The American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA) is the national professional, scientific, and credentialing association for members and affiliates who are audiologists; speech-language pathologists; speech, language, and hearing scientists; audiology and speech-language pathology assistants; and students.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Research on effective interventions for developmental disorders in lower middle-income countries is scarce, limiting evidence-based practices necessary for improving child outcomes.
Interventions must be tailored to local cultural contexts to enhance their effectiveness and acceptance among families, ensuring better engagement and outcomes for children.

Many children in lower middle-income countries face significant barriers to accessing necessary developmental interventions, exacerbated by economic and geographic disparities in healthcare availability.
Local research initiatives are crucial for generating context-specific data, informing effective intervention strategies, and fostering community-based solutions to developmental disorders.
